story:
Jimbo stood under the sign, taking in the place that had somehow followed them from San Diego to Anaheim without changing a thing. Twenty years vanished at the door. His pulse quickened. The last time they’d eaten here had changed everything. The optimist in him said it bought them a decade of happiness; the pessimist whispered that without it they might still be happy.

Mary—no, Meredith now—sat waiting in a booth. She smiled when their eyes met, a rare, unsolicited gift. “James,” she said as he slid in. Not Jimbo, not Jim, not since the affair. He’d hoped her cheerful call meant a fresh start. Her tone said otherwise. Maybe she’d ordered the burgers with divorce papers on the side.

“Meredith,” he answered, playing along.

She let it go, still warm. “I couldn’t believe it when I found this place again. I had to try it before it disappeared. And no matter what we’ve been through, you’re the only one I wanted to experience it with.”

He took her hand, braced for a flinch that didn’t come. “That was a good meal, wasn’t it?”

She laughed. “I don’t remember the taste. Could’ve been awful. It was still the best dinner.”

“I hope you don’t mind, but I already ordered for us,” she said when he reached for a menu.

“Oh?” He waited for the trap.

“I wanted everything like that night. You know?”

He nodded. “That’s what I would’ve ordered anyway. Think it’ll come with the same topping?”

“The waitress didn’t even blink.”

The menu hadn’t changed either—except the prices. The Special Order Burger still offered anything your heart desired, without listing a single topping.

On their first anniversary they’d tested that promise. He’d smirked and asked for a medium-well burger with mayo, bacon, onions—and, in place of lettuce, stacks of hundred-dollar bills. The waitress had nodded. When the plates arrived, real cash crowned his burger. Over $7,000. They’d paid with one bill and left the change.

They kept things light this time, as if talking too hard might break the spell. The waitress came with their food. His burger wore crisp green hundreds. Mary had fish and chips, same as before. He wondered why she hadn’t asked for money herself.

“Looks like more than last time,” she said, watching him move the bills into a neat stack. He breathed in burger and wealth. He’d count later.

She broke a piece of fish, cooled it. “Still hot. Go on. Tell me if it’s good.”

He bit. Delicious, with a bitterness he chalked up to the money’s tang. Worth it.

He noticed a manila envelope buried under her fries. “What’s that?”

“I asked if special orders came with anything besides the burger. They do.”

Divorce papers, he thought, the words thick as he asked, “What’d you get?”

“Nothing that’ll matter to you.”

He raised the burger again—and his hands went numb. The feeling crawled up his arms, stole his body, silenced his mouth. He sagged in the seat, thoughts racing while everything else slowed.

Calmly, Mary slid the envelope out. “Sorry, sweetheart. Your lungs and heart will stop in a minute or two. They promised it would be painless.” A beat. “That’s more than you deserve.”

She opened the envelope, took out a driver’s license and a small slip of paper. She swapped licenses in her wallet. The older one hit the table—and her body changed all at once. The gray vanished, the years fell away. A redhead stared back from his fading vision, young and bright and built to be noticed. Not a trick of the poison. Real.

“I’ll be the prime suspect, so I ordered a new identity—more than a paper trail. It transfers anything in my old name, your name, or ours.” She flicked a credit card, smiled. “Already works.”

She gathered her purse, the envelope, and his stack of cash, leaving two hundreds on the table. She didn’t look back. The waitress caught his eye and shrugged, as if to say, What are you going to do?

His breath turned thin. His heart slowed. In a place that promised anything your heart desired, Mary had ordered a clean escape. He had ordered the burger. He felt the answer arrive as everything stopped.
